paper.title: And then they were two: detection of non-thermal radio emission from the bow shocks of two runaway stars

review.summary: This paper presents radio observations (VLA and Effelsberg) of two runaway-star bow shocks: BD+43°3654 (BSBD43) and BD+60°2522 / NGC 7635 (BSBD60). The data assets include: (1) a literature-compilation table of stellar and bow-shock parameters; (2) an observational flux table at multiple frequencies from interferometric, single-dish, and feathered data; (3) a broadband SED table combining radio fluxes from this work and the literature with X-ray and γ-ray upper limits; (4) a model-parameter table from naima synchrotron/IC fitting. External resources cited include Gaia EDR3 data (via ESA URLs), a HubbleSite image of NGC 7635, and CASA/VLA documentation URLs. No proprietary survey data archives with direct download URLs are described.

external_resources.description: CASA tclean task documentation referenced in a footnote describing the imaging algorithm used for VLA data reduction.

source_refs.context:
In addition, we selected a cell size of \ang{;;0.7} and scales sizes of [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 50, 100, 200] pixels, with a small scale bias 0.9.
The scale size 0 represents a point source, whereas the larger sizes represent wider features that are dominant in the image.
The small scale bias is ``a numerical control to bias the solution towards smaller scales''\footnote{\url{https://casa.nrao.edu/casadocs/casa-5.5.0/global-task-list/task_tclean/about}}.
Scales that were too large were ignored by the software.

external_resources.evidence: Footnote in Section 3.1 explaining the small scale bias parameter used in the tclean task for MS-MFS imaging.

external_resources.description: CASA widebandpbcor task documentation referenced in a footnote describing the primary-beam correction of spectral-index maps.

source_refs.context:
In order to do that, we fed the task with the raw data as well as the basename of our imagefiles and selected the action 
\emph{calcalpha}, 
which recalculates and corrects for PB response only the spectral index map and its error\footnote{\url{https://casa.nrao.edu/casadocs/casa-5.5.0/global-task-list/task_widebandpbcor/about}}.
Next, we applied a mask of 5$\sigma$ (which $\sigma$ for BSBD43 is \SI{0.06}{mJy.beam^{-1}} and \SI{0.2}{mJy.beam^{-1}} for BSBD60) in order to bring forth the most significant areas of emission (Fig.~\ref{fig:bd_spindx_all} top).
We repeated the steps for the spectral index error maps (Fig.~\ref{fig:bd_spindx_all} bottom).

external_resources.evidence: Footnote in Section 3.1 explaining the calcalpha action used to correct spectral index maps for primary beam response.

external_resources.description: Hubble Space Telescope image of NGC 7635 (Bubble Nebula) referenced as an optical comparison to radio morphology.

source_refs.context:
This part is also visible in our single-dish observation (Fig.~\ref{fig:eff_int}, left), but in that case every other detail is not present.

For BSBD60, the details seen in our high-resolution radio image (Fig.~\ref{fig:all_int}, right) correspond very well with the narrow-band nebular emission maps seen in optical with e.g. HST \citepads{2002AJ....124.3313M}\footnote{see also: \url{https://hubblesite.org/contents/media/images/2016/13/3725-Image.html}}.
The bright arc on the North side of the Bubble corresponds to the brightest rim of the optical counterpart, and the bright-rimmed pillar seen projected onto the Bubble is the brightest emission region in radio.
Further bright-rimmed clouds photoionised by \bdsixty to the NNE of the Bubble are also detected with the VLA.

external_resources.evidence: Footnote in Section 4.2 stating that the radio morphology of BSBD60 corresponds well with narrow-band nebular emission maps seen in optical with HST.

external_resources.description: VLA observational performance and resolution documentation referenced when discussing spatial-scale sensitivity limits.

source_refs.context:
This is primarily due to the observing limits of the VLA.
In particular, for our chosen D configuration, for C-band those limits are \ang{;;12}--\ang{;;240}, while for X-band are \ang{;;7.2}--\ang{;;145}.
For sources with emission structures on scales larger than those limits the VLA cannot detect any emission, while for smaller structures the array acts like a single-dish instrument, smoothing the image to a lower resolution\footnote{\url{https://science.nrao.edu/facilities/vla/docs/manuals/oss/performance/resolution}}. 
In our case, BSBD60 is within the observational limits of the array, but BSBD43 is not, resulting in loss of emission and resolution.

external_resources.evidence: Footnote in Section 4.2 explaining the angular-scale limits of the VLA D configuration and why BSBD43 suffers from loss of emission and resolution.
